issues.body: This property returns nothing. As planned?
Looks a bit inconsistent: you explicitly defines POST method, but implicitly GET one. Docs and suggestions will get confused.
OPTIONS is missed. It actually rarely need until you face the CORs thing, but it's quite often happens if you build some web service.
Cookies protocol is a bit weird. May be extract it into separate dict-like class with some more nice API?
Double decode happens there: first for `value = value.decode('utf-8')` second for `decode` lambda in case if charset isn't `utf-8`.
May be respect charset here?
Not finished yet but @fafhrd91 asked me to merge existing branch -- we'll publish it in new release.

Resolve could be more optimal: instead of storing all the routes in list, you can put them in dict and first, look up by method and only after - by path. Also, HTTP 406/415 aren't respected here (which could also optimize lookup complexity).
Do you mean co-existing cookies dict and set_cookie method at the same time?

POST should be coroutine: it reads data from input stream. You can skip reading if you don't need to process POST parameters.
GET is just a dict constructed from query string. Do you like to make GET coroutine also?
I have to take a look twice -- the code was borrowed from WebOb.

OPTIONS added.
I'll transplate CORS support from aiorest a bit later after stabilizing API.
Not sure - I don't know the use case for now, but at least I feel it need to be declared explicitly via `def GET`.

Linear lookup is very simple for both writing and understanding.
If you like some different way I guess to implement another router.
Yes. While all others are full featured properties, the cookies is different. However, not sure how for now, but it could be better. May be figure something later.
Good, as an option. May be I'll have a chance to backport the one I have from another project - it uses the similar logic I described.
For me, it's overkill to use a coroutine to read GET params, they are in the first line of HTTP request, instead of POST payload could be very big, it justifies the usage of a coroutine.
Yocto detail: you use 'utf-8' notation in others places, but 'utf8' in this place.
Copy from WebOb :) I'll change it

Yes, that's the reason for chosen design decision.
But maybe we change it in few days before publishing new aiohttp release -- after the event API should be stable.
So any comments are very appreciated.
